nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still waters of the Congo Basin: heavily vegetated river margins, floodplains, swampy forest streams, and shallow lake edges with abundant leaf litter and submerged woody debris

decorPreferences: dense planting (Amazon sword, Java fern, Anubias, floating plants)
driftwood and root tangles for ambush cover
tight-fitting lid — labyrinth organ requires access to warm humid air

diet.notes: Piscivore-leaning ambush predator. Will consume any tankmate small enough to swallow (roughly up to one-third of own body length). Primary diet in captivity: meaty frozen or live foods — earthworms, large bloodworm, prawn/shrimp pieces, mussel, and feeder insects. Many specimens can be trained over time to accept high-quality carnivore pellets and freeze-dried foods, though this can require patience and consistent target feeding. Live feeder fish should be used cautiously due to disease-introduction risk and the risk of reinforcing live-prey preference. Will not accept plant-based or flake foods.

goodWith: large characins too big to be swallowed
larger peaceful cichlids of comparable body size
robust mid-to-bottom-dwelling fish that won’t outcompete at feeding
avoidWith: any fish smaller than approximately one-third the Ctenopoma’s body length (will be eaten)
small tetras, rasboras, danios, guppies, or nano fish
other Ctenopoma acutirostre (territorial disputes unless in a very large tank)
aggressive or fin-nipping species that harass slow-moving stalkers
invertebrates — shrimp and small snails will be predated

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo. Check availability and licensing before use.

summary: A large, cryptic ambush predator from the Congo Basin with striking leopard-spot patterning. Breathes air via a labyrinth organ but is not a gourami. Requires a tightly lidded, well-planted tank and meaty foods; will consume tankmates up to a third of its own body length.
careNotes:
The Leopard Bush Fish (Ctenopoma acutirostre) is an Anabantoid — it possesses a labyrinth organ enabling it to breathe atmospheric air — but belongs to family Anabantidae, not Osphronemidae, and is unrelated to the gouramis most aquarists associate with labyrinth fish. This distinction matters behaviourally: unlike bubble-nesting gouramis, C. acutirostre is a solitary territorial stalker with no parental care drive outside of spawning.

In the wild it occupies slow or still, heavily vegetated and dimly lit sections of the Congo River Basin, relying on intricate leopard-spotted camouflage to ambush prey. In captivity a spacious tank of 200 L or more with generous planting, driftwood tangles, and floating cover is recommended to let the fish express its natural hunting behaviour and to provide visual breaks that reduce territorial stress. Subdued or ambient lighting suits its preferences and reduces stress; bright, open aquaria should be avoided.

A tight-fitting lid is non-negotiable: the fish must access warm, humid air above the water surface, and it is also a capable jumper when startled. The gap between water surface and lid should ideally be kept warm — cold air drawn over the labyrinth organ can cause respiratory infections.

Feeding can be a challenge with newly acquired specimens. Starting with live earthworms or live feeder insects is often necessary, with a gradual transition to frozen meaty foods (large bloodworm, prawn, mussel) and eventually to quality carnivore sinking pellets using target feeding with tongs or a feeding stick. Do not house with any fish small enough to fit in its mouth — the risk exists even with species the Ctenopoma seems to “ignore” for weeks. Suitable tankmates are large, robust fish that occupy different swimming zones and are not small enough to be predated.

Water chemistry should lean soft and slightly acidic to neutral, reflecting Congo Basin conditions, though the species is reasonably adaptable within the given parameter range. Regular partial water changes maintaining low nitrates are important for long-term health.
breedingNotes: Breeding C. acutirostre in captivity is uncommon but has been achieved by dedicated specialists. Unlike most Anabantoids, the Leopard Bush Fish is not a bubble-nester; it is an egg-scatterer. Spawning is typically triggered by conditioning the pair with copious live and frozen foods and by simulating the onset of the rainy season through large, slightly cooler water changes. Eggs are pelagic and float at the surface, where they must be protected from the parents (which show no parental care post-spawn) and from fungal infection. Fry are extremely small at hatching and require infusoria or commercially prepared micro-fry foods for the first week before graduating to baby brine shrimp and micro-worms. Rearing success is low without dedicated fry tanks. Sexing adults is difficult outside of spawning condition; males may develop small spines on the opercular region (a characteristic of the genus), and well-conditioned females become noticeably rotund.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Ctenopoma acutirostre
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 20 cm in wild, typically 12–15 cm in aquaria), temperature 23–28°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–215 ppm (approx. gH 2–15), native range (Congo Basin), biotope, behaviour notes (ambush predator, egg-scatterer), tank size recommendations (minimum 200 L for adults), and breeding account.

additional.name: FishBase — Ctenopoma acutirostre
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 20 cm TL), taxonomy (Anabantidae, Anabantiformes), native range (Congo basin, DRC), habitat (slow/still waters), diet (invertebrates, small fish), and depth range. Confirmed labyrinth organ presence and family classification distinct from Osphronemidae.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Leopard Bush Fish Care Guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist-oriented care guide. Confirmed care level (intermediate), minimum tank size (~55 gal / ~208 L), predatory nature (eats fish up to 1/3 its size), feeding transition from live to frozen/prepared foods, lid requirement, and compatibility guidelines. Temperature and pH parameters consistent with SeriouslyFish.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Ctenopoma acutirostre profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source confirming egg-scattering breeding mode (atypical among Anabantoids), solitary territorial behaviour, Congo Basin origin, and labyrinth organ classification within Anabantidae. Also noted the challenge of distinguishing sexes outside breeding condition.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer, unusual among Anabantoids. Pelagic eggs require separation from parents immediately post-spawn. Fry are tiny and need infusoria initially. Conditioning with live/frozen foods and simulated rainy-season water changes is typically required to trigger spawning. Rarely bred in captivity.
